text:
*[Bug 19080] New: getGenerics() returns package names, not generics*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19080>

            Bug ID: 19080
           Summary: getGenerics() returns package names, not generics
           Product: R
           Version: R-devel (trunk)
          Hardware: All
                OS: All
            Status: NEW
          Severity: normal
          Priority: P5
         Component: S4methods
          Assignee: <mailto:R-core@R-project.org|R-core@R-project.org>
          Reporter: <mailto:michaelchirico4@gmail.com|michaelchirico4@gmail.com>

```
library(Matrix)
library(S4Vectors) # Bioconductor
```

With this, `getGenerics()` starts "leaking" package names into the result:

```
c("Matrix", "S4Vectors", "base") %in% getGenerics()
# [1] TRUE TRUE TRUE
```

Discovered (by Gemini) while working on revdep checks for Bug #19079, where a
natural thing to try is:

```
for (g in getGenerics()) {
  methods <- findMethods(g)
  # ...
}
```

But in the above session, we'll get warnings:

```
for (g in getGenerics()) findMethods(g)
# Warning messages:
# 1: In findMethods(g) :
#   non-generic function 'Matrix' given to findMethods()
# 2: In findMethods(g) :
#   non-generic function 'S4Vectors' given to findMethods()
# 3: In findMethods(g) : non-generic function 'base' given to findMethods()
# 4: In findMethods(g) :
#   non-generic function 'S4Vectors' given to findMethods()
```

text:
*[Bug 19080] getGenerics() can return package names in addition to generics*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19080>

--- Comment #1 from Michael Chirico (<mailto:michaelchirico4@gmail.com|michaelchirico4@gmail.com>) ---
Created attachment 3718
  --> <https://bugs.r-project.org/attachment.cgi?id=3718&action=edit>
Fix handling of multiply-defined generics in getGenerics()

This looks like a plain bug: when a generic is defined more than once, the
corresponding element in `.genericTable` is a `list()` with structure:
`list(PACKAGE.1 = <standardGenericObject>, PACKAGE.2 = <standardGenericObject>,
...)`. The current code takes `names()`, which gives back `c("PACKAGE.1",
"PACKAGE.2", ...)`, where the code is actually looking for the _generic_ name,
not the _package_ name.

I fixed this with a helper designed analogously to `.packageForGeneric` which
is used nearby, I think this is the best shot to ensure the codepaths for both
generic _names_ and host _packages_ stay in sync.

I found that my choice of name for this helper, `.genericName`, conflicted with
another helper from the same file that's no longer used, so I deleted the other
one.

I also had Gemini write a regression test, which I touched up lightly for
conciseness. The idea is simple enough: make two minimal test packages, each
defining a generic with the same name, and show how `getGenerics()` behaves on
a subprocess attaching both of them. I leaned on Gemini to try and match the
prevailing style of tests nearby. One unfortunate discovery is that
`package.skeleton()` itself has the side effect of registering S4 methods,
which causes an issue when the second execution finds a conflicting generic, so
we first take pains to erase the first package's generic, in a way where
`removeGeneric()` can't help us.

GHA testing: <https://github.com/r-devel/r-svn/pull/260>

text:
*[Bug 19068] `dev.capabilities()` reports wrong transparency values for BMP/JPEG/TIFF formats for cairo and Xlib types*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19068>

--- Comment #5 from Trevor Davis (<mailto:trevor.l.davis@gmail.com|trevor.l.davis@gmail.com>) ---
Unfortunately you got to actually examine the actual pixel values instead of
glancing at the images to tell the difference between pixels like `#0000FF80`
(semi-transparent blue on a white window) and `#000080FF` (opaque light blue).

Here is some code to generate images separately testing:

1. semi-transparent background support
2. semi-transparency support (composited onto an opaque background)

```
library("grDevices")
library("grid")

for (fname in c("bmp", "jpeg", "tiff")) {
        for (type in c("cairo", "Xlib")) {
                fn <- getFromNamespace(fname, "grDevices")

                file <- sprintf("bg_%s.%s", type, fname)
                fn(file, bg = "#0000FF80", type = type)
                grid.newpage()
                invisible(dev.off())

                file <- sprintf("semi_%s.%s", type, fname)
                fn(file, bg = "white", type = type)
                grid.rect(gp = gpar(fill = "#0000FF80", col = NA))
                invisible(dev.off())
        }
}
```

Here is some code to grab the relevant pixels:

```
# If semi-transparent background is supported should be #0000FF80
as.raster(magick::image_read("bg_cairo.bmp"))[100L, 100L]
as.raster(jpeg::readJPEG("bg_cairo.jpeg"))[100L, 100L]
as.raster(tiff::readTIFF("bg_cairo.tiff"))[100L, 100L]

# If semi-transparent background is supported should be #0000FF80
as.raster(magick::image_read("bg_Xlib.bmp"))[100L, 100L]
as.raster(jpeg::readJPEG("bg_Xlib.jpeg"))[100L, 100L]
as.raster(tiff::readTIFF("bg_Xlib.tiff"))[100L, 100L]

# #0000FF80 rectangle composited onto #FFFFFFFF background
# If semi-transparency is supported should be close to #8080FFFF
as.raster(magick::image_read("semi_cairo.bmp"))[100L, 100L]
as.raster(jpeg::readJPEG("semi_cairo.jpeg"))[100L, 100L]
as.raster(tiff::readTIFF("semi_cairo.tiff"))[100L, 100L]
```

Here is some more information on `tiff(type = "cairo")` which although it does
save a semi-transparent background however it doesn't do it "properly":

1. Internally cairo renders the bitmap with *pre-multiplied* alpha which the
tiff pathway then saves as is.  However the format expects instead *straight*
alpha so to properly support it you'd need to convert the Cairo pre-multiplied
alpha to straight alpha before saving.
2. If there are semi-transparent pixels you are also supposed to set an
"extrasamples" tag in the tiff file (but this doesn't happen).  Note when
`tiff::readTIFF()` tries to read it it emits a warning.

tiff pathway then saves as is.  However the format expects instead

"extrasamples" tag in the tiff file (but this doesn't happen).  Note when
tries to read it it emits a warning.

No answer yet, but for the record, the code in allocationRemaining() was itself
a bug fix, so is deliberate (though not necessarily correct) ...

<https://github.com/r-devel/r-svn/commit/a3bd91bfd2a906650db26d6d5bee211b4bbbd8e5>

Unfortunately, as that bug fix points out, we have to allow for the possibility
of a negative viewport width/height, which does not make it any easier to think
about what the right behaviour should be :(

text:
*[Bug 19081] New: `x11()` keyboard events doesn't correctly handle "Home", "Delete", and Keypad when Num Lock is off*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19081>

            Bug ID: 19081
           Summary: `x11()` keyboard events doesn't correctly handle
                    "Home", "Delete", and Keypad when Num Lock is off
           Product: R
           Version: R-devel (trunk)
          Hardware: Other
                OS: Linux
            Status: UNCONFIRMED
          Severity: normal
          Priority: P5
         Component: I/O
          Assignee: <mailto:R-core@R-project.org|R-core@R-project.org>
          Reporter: <mailto:trevor.l.davis@gmail.com|trevor.l.davis@gmail.com>

Created attachment 3719
  --> <https://bugs.r-project.org/attachment.cgi?id=3719&action=edit>
Correctly handle "Home", "Delete", and Keypad when Num Lock is off

The following code helps test keyboard events in interactive `x11()` and
`windows()` devices:

```
keystroke <- function(key) {
    cp <- utf8ToInt(key)
    if (length(cp) == 1L) {
        if (grepl("[[:space:][:cntrl:]]", key)) {
            cat(sprintf("keystroke   (U+%04X)\n", cp))
        } else {
            cat(sprintf("keystroke   key=%s  (U+%04X)\n", key, cp))
        }
    } else {
        cat(sprintf("keystroke   key=%s\n", key))
    }
    NULL
}
if (identical(.Platform$OS.type, "unix")) {
    x11(type = "Xlib")
} else {
    windows()
}
par(mar = c(0, 0, 0, 0))
plot.new()
getGraphicsEvent(prompt = "Press keys to test (close window to quit)",
                 onKeybd = keystroke)
```

I observe the following issues when testing the keyboard events in `x11()`:

1.  According to ?grDevices::getGraphicsEvent the "Home" key is supposed to be
passed as "Home" but instead doesn't trigger an event at all.
2. According to ?grDevices::getGraphicsEvent the "Delete" key is supposed to be
passed as "Del" but instead is passed as `"\u007f"` (Unicode code point for
Delete).
3. There are several Navigation and Edit keys available on numeric keypads when
the "Num Lock" is off.  I observe that they don't trigger an event at all.

In contrast when testing with `windows()` 1, 2, and 3 all work as "expected".

I've attached a patch that should fix 1, 2, and 3 in `x11()`.  

Note currently the "Begin" key triggers a "Home " value (and the "Home" key
doesn't trigger anything).  For backwards compatibility I have both the "Begin"
key and "Home" key trigger a "Home" value.  Seemingly there was an old terminal
that had separate keys to distinguish "Home" (beginning of file) from "Begin"
(beginning of line) but most modern keyboards only support "Home" so it may
make sense instead to delete that "Begin" key to "Home" value mapping and only
support a "Home" key to "Home" value mapping.

I also note that the following five keys return the following Unicode values
(instead of special string values like "Esc", "Enter", and "Tab").  What they
should return isn't specified in the ?getGraphicsEvent and the Unicode values
they do return in `x11()` seem sensible (and in contrast for these following
five values `windows()` is currently buggy):

| Name | Value |
|---|---|
| Backspace | `"\u0008"` (Backspace control) |
| Enter | `"\u000d"` (i.e. `"\r"`) |
| Escape | `"\u001b"` (Escape control) |
| Space | `"\u0020"` (i.e. `" "`) |
| Tab | `"\u0009"` (i.e. `"\t"`) |
